comments.text: I wrote a short note giving an explicit lower bound for the liminf in Problem #390. It should be noted that this result was obtained with AI:\[ \liminf_{n\to\infty} \frac{f(n)-2n}{n/\log n} \ge \frac{4029639598}{25970038185} \approx 0.15516494697830188. \]This does not prove an upper bound, nor does it prove that an asymptotic constant exists. It is just a finite lower-bound improvement, using the first thirteen large-prime obstruction layers. The argument is closely related to the large-prime obstruction Tao described in his earlier comment, but is presented here as a finite, quantified version. Thanks. PDF: https://drive.google.com/file/d/1gcFkDf-6PIIjpZAYC9rqfhWf5IKgL218/view?usp=sharing
Congrats. Standard check found no issues. The previously known lower bound value from the notes and also from quick literature search was $1/9$.

This problem shares many features with #391, and in particular many of the techniques in this recent paper of Alexeev, Conway, Rosenfeld, Sutherland, Uhr, Ventullo, and myself may be relevant here. One can summarize the arguments in [EGS82] as follows. In both the upper and lower bound one should think of the product $a_1 \dots a_k$ by starting from $(n+1) \dots 2n = n! \binom{2n}{n}$, removing some factors $b_1,\dots,b_r$ in $(n,2n]$, and then adding some factors $c_1,\dots,c_s$ in $(2n, f(n)]$. Thus, one can rewrite the equation $n! = a_1 \dots a_k$ as $$ b_1 \dots b_r = \binom{2n}{n} c_1 \dots c_s \quad (1)$$ for some $n < b_1 < \dots < b_r \leq 2n$ and $2n < c_1 < \dots < c_s \leq f(n)$. For the lower bound: every prime $p$ in $(2n/4, 2n/3]$ divides $\binom{2n}{n}$ once (by Kummer's theorem), which means exactly one of $2p$ or $3p$ has to be in the $\{b_1,\dots,b_r\}$. By the prime number theorem, this creates $\gg \frac{n}{\log n}$ factors of $2$ or $3$ on the LHS of (1); since $\binom{2n}{n}$ only has $O(\log n)$ factors of each (by Kummer again), these factors have to be absorbed by the $c_1,\dots,c_s$; but $(2n,f(n)]$ only contains $O(f(n)-2n + \log n)$ copies of both $2$ and $3$, which easily leads to the lower bound $f(n)-2n \gg \frac{n}{\log n}$ (assuming $n$ large enough of course). For the upper bound: factorize $\binom{2n}{n}$ into prime powers $p^a$; one can check (again from Kummer) that all such factors are at most $2n$. For each such prime power, add $2^i p^a$ to the collection $b_1,\dots,b_r$, where $i$ is chosen so that $2^i p^a \in (n,2n]$. This balances out all the factors of $\binom{2n}{n}$, but creates excess powers of 2 on the LHS; one can calculate this excess (using the prime number theorem) to be $O(\frac{n}{\log n})$. To balance out this excess, add various $b_j$ to the LHS and $2b_j$ to the RHS for greedily chosen $n < b_j < n + O(n/\log n)$; this will establish $f(n)-2n \ll \frac{n}{\log n}$. The lower bound analysis is wasteful because it does not take into account the primes $p \in (2n/6, 2n/5]$, $p \in (2n/8, 2n/7]$, etc. (though the first few intervals should provide the dominant contribution to the bound). The upper bound analysis is wasteful because it only uses even numbers for the $c_j$ to balance powers of 2 on the LHS; one should also be able to balance powers of 3, 5, etc. with more effort. One key idea in the paper linked above is that products of consecutive integers $\prod_{j=1}^H (N+j)$ have prime factorizations that do not depend very strongly on the starting point $N$: the number of times a prime $p$ divides $\prod_{j=1}^H (N+j)$ is almost the same as the number of times it divides $\prod_{j=1}^H (N'+j)$ if $p$ is small compared to $N,N',H$. This should allow one to introduce blocks of consecutive integers on the left and right-hand sides of (1) to help balance the prime factorizations, though actually carrying out this strategy could be complicated.
